    Console.WriteLine(*ptr); // 42
}

followUps.question: What is MSIL / CIL and how does the CLR execute it?
followUps.answer: When you compile C# code, the compiler (csc/Roslyn) does NOT produce native machine code. It produces Microsoft Intermediate Language (MSIL), also called Common Intermediate Language (CIL) — a CPU-independent bytecode format stored in a .dll or .exe assembly. At runtime, the CLR's JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler translates MSIL to native machine code on the fly the first time each method is called, then caches the result. This is why .NET apps start slightly slower but reach near-native speed once warmed up. .NET 6+ also ships ReadyToRun (R2R) — pre-JIT compilation done at publish time to reduce startup latency.

followUps.question: What are the benefits of managed code over unmanaged code?
followUps.answer:
Benefits of managed code:

  • Automatic memory management — GC handles heap allocation and collection; no memory leaks from forgetting to free memory.
  • Type safety — CLR verifies types at runtime, preventing type confusion attacks and pointer errors.
  • Exception handling — structured try/catch/finally across the entire call stack.
  • Security — CLR enforces code access security.
  • Cross-language interoperability — C# code can call VB.NET code seamlessly because both compile to the same CIL.

When you'd choose unmanaged: game engines (raw performance), device drivers, or integrating with legacy COM/Win32 systems that have no managed wrapper.

questions.tip: Interviewer shortcut: 'Managed = CLR controls memory; Unmanaged = you control memory'. The CLR's garbage collector is the defining difference.

questions.question: What is boxing and unboxing in C#? What are the performance implications?
questions.answer:
C# has two kinds of types: value types (int, double, bool, struct, enum) that live on the stack, and reference types (class, string, object) that live on the heap.

Boxing is the implicit conversion of a value type to object (or to an interface type it implements). The CLR wraps the value in a new heap object.

Unboxing is the explicit cast from object back to the value type. If the cast doesn't match the original type, an InvalidCastException is thrown at runtime.

int number = 42;          // value type on stack
object boxed = number;    // BOXING — heap allocation, value copied into object wrapper
int unboxed = (int)boxed; // UNBOXING — cast back, value copied out

// InvalidCastException — unboxed type must match original
double bad = (double)boxed; // throws! original was int, not double

Performance implications:

  • Every boxing operation allocates a new object on the heap → GC pressure.
  • Unboxing requires a type check + copy.
  • In hot loops, repeated boxing can be a serious bottleneck.
// BAD — ArrayList boxes every int
var list = new ArrayList();
for (int i = 0; i < 1_000_000; i++)
    list.Add(i); // 1M boxing operations!

// GOOD — List<T> is generic, no boxing
var list = new List<int>();
for (int i = 0; i < 1_000_000; i++)
    list.Add(i); // zero boxing

followUps.question: Where does implicit boxing happen without you realising it?
followUps.answer:
Boxing happens silently in these common scenarios:

  • Passing a value type to a method that accepts object: Console.WriteLine(42) — 42 gets boxed.
  • Storing value types in non-generic collections (ArrayList, Hashtable).
  • Calling an interface method on a value type through an interface variable: IComparable c = 42; boxes the int.
  • String interpolation / string.Format before .NET 6's improved overloads: $"Value: {myInt}" historically boxed the int.
  • Using object as a generic constraint instead of T.

The fix in all cases is to use generics (List<T>, generic interfaces) or ensure overloads exist for the concrete value type.

questions.tip: Key rule: Generics (List<T>, Dictionary<K,V>) were invented largely to eliminate boxing. If you see ArrayList or non-generic collections in modern C#, that's a code smell.

questions.question: What is the difference between a namespace and an assembly in .NET?
questions.answer:
These are two completely different concepts that are often confused because they look similar in code.

Namespace

A namespace is a logical grouping of types to avoid name collisions and organise code. It exists only at the source-code and metadata level — it has no physical boundary at runtime.

  • Declared with the namespace keyword.
  • The same namespace can span multiple files, multiple projects, and even multiple assemblies.
  • Referenced with using System.Collections.Generic;.

Assembly

An assembly is a physical deployment and versioning unit — a .dll or .exe file. It is the actual output of building a project.

  • Contains compiled MSIL code, metadata, and embedded resources.
  • Has a unique identity: name + version + culture + public key token (for strong-named assemblies).
  • The CLR loads, versions, and security-checks at the assembly level.
  • A single assembly can contain multiple namespaces.
  • A single namespace can be split across multiple assemblies (e.g., System.Collections is in mscorlib.dll and System.dll).
// Namespace — logical grouping
namespace MyApp.Services
{
    public class EmailService { }
    public class SmsService { }
}

// Assembly — the .dll file
// MyApp.Services.dll (built from the project)
// Can contain namespaces: MyApp.Services, MyApp.Models, etc.

followUps.question: What is the Global Assembly Cache (GAC)?
followUps.answer: The GAC is a machine-wide cache for shared .NET Framework assemblies. When multiple applications need the same assembly (e.g., System.dll), a single copy in the GAC is shared rather than each app bundling its own copy. Assemblies in the GAC must be strong-named (signed with a public/private key pair) to guarantee authenticity. In modern .NET Core / .NET 5+, the GAC is largely gone — each app bundles its own copy of runtime libraries (either self-contained publish, or via a shared runtime installation in a versioned folder). This eliminates the classic .NET Framework "DLL Hell" problem where a shared GAC assembly update could break unrelated apps.

questions.tip: One-liner: Namespace = folder (logical), Assembly = DLL file (physical). You can have the same namespace in different DLLs, and a DLL can contain many namespaces.

questions.question: What are the ref and out keywords in C#? When do you use each?
questions.answer:
Both ref and out allow a method to modify a variable in the caller's scope by passing a reference to it, rather than a copy.

ref

  • The variable must be initialised before passing.
  • The method may or may not modify it — it's optional.
  • Used when the method needs to read the current value and potentially update it.

out

  • The variable does not need to be initialised before passing (the compiler enforces that the method assigns it before returning).
  • The method must assign the out parameter before the method returns.
  • Used when the method produces an output value, like int.TryParse.
// ref — must be assigned before call, method may read+write
void Increment(ref int value)
{
    value += 1; // reads current value and updates it
}
int x = 10;
Increment(ref x); // x is now 11

// out — no prior assignment needed, must be assigned inside method
bool TryGetUser(int id, out User user)
{
    user = _db.Find(id); // must assign before return
    return user != null;
}
// Caller — out variable declared inline (C# 7+)
if (TryGetUser(1, out User found))
    Console.WriteLine(found.Name);

// Classic real-world example
if (int.TryParse("123", out int result))
    Console.WriteLine(result); // 123

Key difference table:

  • ref: caller must initialise → method may read/write
  • out: caller need not initialise → method must write

followUps.question: Can you overload a method by changing only ref/out?
followUps.answer: No — ref and out are not part of the method signature for the purposes of overload resolution between each other. You can have one overload with ref and one without, but you cannot have one with ref and one with out for the same parameter type — the compiler considers them the same signature and reports an error. Also, you cannot use ref and out with async methods or iterator methods (yield return).

followUps.question: What is the 'in' keyword in C#? How is it different from ref?
followUps.answer:
The in keyword (C# 7.2+) passes a variable by reference but marks it as read-only — the method cannot modify it. It's mainly a performance optimisation for large structs: instead of copying a 200-byte struct on every call, you pass a reference but guarantee immutability. ref: read + write. in: read only. out: write only (from caller perspective).

void Display(in LargeStruct s)
{
    Console.WriteLine(s.Name); // fine
    s.Name = "new"; // compile error — cannot modify 'in' parameter
}

questions.tip: Memory aid: out = output (the method gives you something). ref = reference (two-way, you give something, method may change it). The classic use of out is the TryXxx pattern.

questions.question: What are the different types of constructors in C#?
questions.answer:
A constructor is a special method that initialises an object. C# supports several types:

  1. Default Constructor — no parameters. If you don't define any constructor, C# generates one automatically. Once you define a parameterized constructor, the default is no longer auto-generated.
  2. Parameterized Constructor — accepts arguments to set initial state.
  3. Copy Constructor — takes an instance of the same type and copies its values. C# has no built-in copy constructor (unlike C++), but you write one manually.
  4. Static Constructor — no access modifier, no parameters, runs once before any instance is created or any static member is accessed. Used to initialise static fields.
  5. Private Constructor — prevents direct instantiation. Used in Singleton pattern and factory methods.
public class Person
{
    public string Name { get; set; }
    public int Age  { get; set; }

    // 1. Default constructor
    public Person() { Name = "Unknown"; }

    // 2. Parameterized constructor
    public Person(string name, int age) { Name = name; Age = age; }

    // 3. Copy constructor (manual)
    public Person(Person other) { Name = other.Name; Age = other.Age; }

    // 4. Static constructor
    static Person() { Console.WriteLine("Person type loaded"); }

    // 5. Private constructor (used in Singleton)
    private Person(bool singleton) { }
    public static Person Instance { get; } = new Person(true);
}

// Constructor chaining with : this()
public Person(string name) : this(name, 0) { }

followUps.question: What is constructor chaining and why is it useful?
followUps.answer:
Constructor chaining calls one constructor from another in the same class using : this() syntax, or calls the parent class constructor using : base(). It avoids duplicate initialisation logic.

public class Order
{
    public int Id { get; }
    public string Status { get; }
    public DateTime CreatedAt { get; }

    // Primary constructor with all logic
    public Order(int id, string status, DateTime createdAt)
    {
        Id = id; Status = status; CreatedAt = createdAt;
    }

    // Convenience overloads chain to the primary
    public Order(int id) : this(id, "Pending", DateTime.UtcNow) { }
    public Order(int id, string status) : this(id, status, DateTime.UtcNow) { }
}

Using : base(args) calls the parent class constructor before the child's body runs, ensuring the parent is properly initialised first.

questions.tip: Static constructor gotcha: You can't control when a static constructor runs (CLR decides), you can't call it explicitly, and if it throws, the type becomes unusable for the lifetime of the AppDomain.

questions.question: What are the different uses of the 'using' keyword in C#?
questions.answer:
The using keyword has four distinct uses in C#:

1. using directive — namespace import

using System.Collections.Generic; // import namespace
using static System.Math;         // import static members (C# 6)
using Alias = System.Text.StringBuilder; // type alias

2. using statement — deterministic disposal (C# 1-7)

Creates a scope; calls Dispose() on the object when the block exits — even if an exception is thrown. Requires the type to implement IDisposable.

using (var conn = new SqlConnection(connStr))
using (var cmd  = new SqlCommand(sql, conn))
{
    conn.Open();
    cmd.ExecuteNonQuery();
} // Dispose() called on both, in reverse order

3. using declaration — scope-based disposal (C# 8+)

No braces needed; the object is disposed at the end of the enclosing scope.

void ProcessFile(string path)
{
    using var reader = new StreamReader(path);
    string content = reader.ReadToEnd();
    // reader.Dispose() called at end of method
}

4. global using — project-wide import (C# 10+)

// In GlobalUsings.cs — applies to every file in the project
global using System.Text.Json;
global using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;

followUps.question: What happens if Dispose() throws an exception inside a using block?
followUps.answer: If the body of the using block throws an exception and Dispose() also throws, the original exception is lost — only the Dispose exception propagates. This is a known C# design trade-off. Best practice: write Dispose() so it never throws (catch any internal exceptions and log them instead). This is one reason the IDisposable pattern typically has a try/catch inside Dispose(bool disposing).

questions.tip: Rule of thumb: Any class that holds unmanaged resources (file handles, DB connections, network sockets) should implement IDisposable, and callers should always wrap it in using.

questions.question: What is early binding and late binding in C#? Give real examples.
questions.answer:
Early binding (static binding) — the compiler resolves which method to call at compile time. The exact method is baked into the IL. This is the default for all regular method calls.

Late binding (dynamic binding) — the method to call is resolved at runtime, not compile time. Used with virtual methods, interfaces, and the dynamic keyword.

Early binding example — method call resolved at compile time:

var sb = new StringBuilder();
sb.Append("hello"); // Compiler knows exactly which Append overload to call
// Fast — direct method call in IL

Late binding via virtual dispatch (polymorphism):

Animal animal = new Dog(); // reference is Animal, object is Dog
animal.Speak();            // LATE BINDING — resolved at runtime via vtable
// Which Speak() runs? Dog.Speak() — not Animal.Speak()
// The runtime checks the actual object type

public class Animal { public virtual void Speak() => Console.WriteLine("..."); }
public class Dog : Animal  { public override void Speak() => Console.WriteLine("Woof!"); }

Late binding via 'dynamic' keyword:

dynamic obj = GetSomething(); // could be any type
obj.DoWork();                 // resolved at runtime using DLR
// No compile-time checking — if DoWork() doesn't exist, RuntimeBinderException at runtime

Late binding via Reflection:

Type type = Type.GetType("MyApp.Services.EmailService");
var instance = Activator.CreateInstance(type);
type.GetMethod("SendEmail").Invoke(instance, new object[] { "hi" });

Performance: Early binding = fast. Late binding (especially reflection/dynamic) = slower due to runtime type resolution overhead.

followUps.question: How does virtual method dispatch (vtable) work?
followUps.answer: Every class with virtual methods has a vtable (virtual dispatch table) — an array of function pointers, one per virtual method. When an object is created, its vtable pointer is set to the vtable of its actual type. When you call animal.Speak() on a Dog instance through an Animal reference, the CLR looks up Speak in Dog's vtable and calls it — regardless of what the reference variable's declared type is. This is how polymorphism works at the machine level. The lookup adds one level of indirection compared to a direct (early-bound) call, but in practice the overhead is negligible.

questions.tip: Interview link: Late binding via virtual dispatch = polymorphism. Late binding via dynamic/reflection = runtime flexibility at the cost of type safety and performance.

questions.question: Can an abstract class have a parameterized constructor? How do you call it from a derived class?
questions.answer:
Yes — abstract classes can have constructors, including parameterized ones. Constructors in abstract classes are used to initialise fields/properties that belong to the abstract class itself. Since you can never directly instantiate an abstract class (new Animal() is a compile error), its constructor is always called indirectly through a derived class.

How the derived class calls it — using : base()

public abstract class Shape
{
    public string Color { get; }
    public int BorderWidth { get; }

    // Parameterized constructor on the abstract class
    protected Shape(string color, int borderWidth)
    {
        Color = color;
        BorderWidth = borderWidth;
    }

    public abstract double Area(); // must be overridden
}

public class Circle : Shape
{
    public double Radius { get; }

    // Derived class MUST call a base constructor via : base(...)
    public Circle(double radius, string color, int borderWidth)
        : base(color, borderWidth) // calls Shape's constructor
    {
        Radius = radius;
    }

    public override double Area() => Math.PI * Radius * Radius;
}

// Usage
var c = new Circle(5.0, "Red", 2);
Console.WriteLine(c.Color);  // "Red" — set by Shape's constructor via base()
Console.WriteLine(c.Area()); // 78.54

Key rules:

  • If the abstract class has only a parameterized constructor (no default), all derived classes must call : base(...) explicitly.
  • The : base() call always runs before the derived class constructor body.
  • Abstract class constructors are typically protected (no point making them public since the class can't be instantiated directly).

followUps.question: What is the order of constructor execution in an inheritance chain?
followUps.answer:
Constructors run from the top of the hierarchy down — the base class constructor always completes before the derived class constructor body runs. For a three-level chain A → B → C:

  1. C's constructor is called
  2. Before C's body runs, : base() invokes B's constructor
  3. Before B's body runs, : base() invokes A's constructor
  4. A's body executes first
  5. Then B's body
  6. Finally C's body

This guarantees that base class invariants are established before the derived class can use them.

questions.tip: Common mistake: Forgetting that if you add a parameterized constructor to an abstract base class without keeping a default constructor, all existing derived classes will fail to compile until they add : base(...).

questions.question: Can a class implement two interfaces that have the same method name? How do you handle conflicts?
questions.answer:
Yes — a class can implement two interfaces with the same method signature. There are two ways to handle it:

Option 1: Single implicit implementation (satisfies both)

If both interfaces require the exact same contract, one method body satisfies both.

interface IReader { string Read(); }
interface IWriter { string Read(); } // same signature

public class DataProcessor : IReader, IWriter
{
    public string Read() => "data"; // one method satisfies both interfaces
}

DataProcessor dp = new DataProcessor();
dp.Read();                   // calls the single Read()
((IReader)dp).Read();        // same method
((IWriter)dp).Read();        // same method

Option 2: Explicit interface implementation (different behaviour per interface)

Prefix the method name with the interface name. Explicitly implemented methods are only accessible through the interface reference — they are hidden on the class type.

interface ILogger  { void Log(string msg); }
interface IAuditor { void Log(string msg); }

public class AuditLogger : ILogger, IAuditor
{
    void ILogger.Log(string msg)  => Console.WriteLine($"[LOG] {msg}");
    void IAuditor.Log(string msg) => Console.WriteLine($"[AUDIT] {msg}");
}

AuditLogger al = new AuditLogger();
// al.Log("test");        // compile error — not accessible on concrete type
((ILogger)al).Log("test");  // [LOG] test
((IAuditor)al).Log("test"); // [AUDIT] test

Explicit interface implementation — other use cases:

  • Hiding interface members that don't make sense on the public API of the class.
  • Implementing IEnumerable.GetEnumerator() (non-generic) alongside IEnumerable<T>.GetEnumerator().

followUps.question: Can an interface have a default implementation (default interface methods)?
followUps.answer:
Yes, since C# 8, interfaces can have default method implementations. This allows adding new methods to existing interfaces without breaking all implementing classes.

interface IShape
{
    double Area();
    // Default implementation — implementing classes can override or use this
    string Describe() => $"This shape has area {Area():F2}";
}

class Circle : IShape
{
    public double Radius { get; set; }
    public double Area() => Math.PI * Radius * Radius;
    // No need to implement Describe() — uses the default
}

IShape s = new Circle { Radius = 3 };
Console.WriteLine(s.Describe()); // works via default implementation

Default interface methods are only accessible through the interface reference, not the concrete type.

questions.tip: Explicit implementation rule: The method is only accessible through the interface reference. This is intentional — it keeps the class's public API clean while still fulfilling the interface contract.

questions.question: What is the difference between Array and ArrayList in C#? When do you use List<T>?
questions.answer:
These three types all store sequences of items, but they differ significantly in type safety, flexibility, and performance.

Array (int[], string[])

  • Fixed size — set at creation, cannot grow or shrink.
  • Strongly typed — only the declared element type allowed.
  • Zero boxing for value types.
  • Fastest random access — O(1), contiguous memory.
  • Use when size is known upfront and doesn't change.

ArrayList (System.Collections)

  • Dynamic size — grows automatically.
  • Stores object — not type-safe, requires casting to retrieve elements.
  • Boxing for every value type — storing an int boxes it to object.
  • Legacy type from .NET 1.x — avoid in modern code.

List<T> (System.Collections.Generic)

  • Dynamic size — like ArrayList, grows automatically (doubles capacity).
  • Strongly typed — no casting, no boxing for value types.
  • Best of both worlds — type safety + dynamic sizing.
  • The default choice for most collections in modern C#.
// Array — fixed, typed
int[] arr = new int[3] { 1, 2, 3 };
arr[0] = 99; // O(1)
// arr[3] = 4; // IndexOutOfRangeException — can't grow

// ArrayList — dynamic, but untyped (avoid)
var al = new ArrayList();
al.Add(42);         // boxes int → object
al.Add("oops");     // no compile error — mixed types allowed
int n = (int)al[0]; // must cast — risky

// List<T> — dynamic + typed (use this)
var list = new List<int>();
list.Add(42);    // no boxing
list.Add(43);
// list.Add("x"); // compile error — type-safe
int x = list[0]; // no cast needed

followUps.question: When is it better to use an array over List<T>?
followUps.answer:
Prefer arrays when:

  • The size is fixed and known (e.g., RGB values — always 3 elements).
  • You need maximum performance — arrays avoid the overhead of List's internal size checks.
  • Working with interop / unsafe code that requires contiguous memory.
  • Multidimensional data — int[,] or int[][] are natural; List of Lists is clunky.
  • Spans and Memory<T> — Span<T> works directly over arrays for zero-copy slicing.

In most application-level code, List<T> is simpler and the performance difference is negligible. Use arrays in hot paths, parsing, serialisation, or when returning from a method where the caller shouldn't modify the size.

questions.tip: Interview shortcut: ArrayList = legacy, untyped, causes boxing. List<T> = modern, typed, no boxing. Use List<T> always unless you need a fixed-size typed array.

questions.question: What is the difference between virtual/override and the new keyword for method hiding in C#?
questions.answer:
This is a classic C# polymorphism question. Both allow a derived class to provide a different implementation of a base class method, but they behave differently when calling through a base reference.

virtual + override — true polymorphism (runtime dispatch)

  • The base method is marked virtual and the derived provides override.
  • Calling through a base type reference calls the derived implementation — because the vtable is used for dispatch.
public class Animal  { public virtual  void Speak() => Console.WriteLine("..."); }
public class Dog : Animal { public override void Speak() => Console.WriteLine("Woof"); }

Animal a = new Dog();
a.Speak(); // "Woof" — override participates in virtual dispatch

new — method hiding (compile-time resolution)

  • The derived class declares a new method with the same name using the new keyword.
  • This hides the base method — it does NOT override it.
  • Calling through a base type reference calls the base implementation — the new method is invisible through the base reference.
public class Animal  { public void Speak() => Console.WriteLine("..."); }
public class Dog : Animal { public new void Speak() => Console.WriteLine("Woof"); }

Dog   d = new Dog();    d.Speak(); // "Woof" — calls Dog.Speak()
Animal a = new Dog();   a.Speak(); // "..."  — calls Animal.Speak() — hiding, NOT polymorphism!

The critical difference:

Scenariooverridenew
Called via derived reference (Dog d)DerivedDerived
Called via base reference (Animal a)Derived ✅Base ❌

followUps.question: What happens if you use 'new' without the keyword explicitly?
followUps.answer: If you define a method in a derived class with the same signature as a base class method, without marking it override or new, C# will compile it but issue a compiler warning: 'Dog.Speak()' hides inherited member 'Animal.Speak()'. Use the new keyword if hiding was intended. The behaviour is the same as new — the base method is hidden. The compiler warning is C#'s way of saying "this looks unintentional". Adding new explicitly silences the warning and documents the intent.

questions.tip: Key interview question: 'Animal a = new Dog(); a.Speak(); what prints?' If Speak is override → Dog's version. If it's new → Animal's version. This tests whether you understand runtime dispatch vs compile-time resolution.

questions.question: What are extension methods in C#? How do you create one and when do you use them?
questions.answer:
Extension methods let you add new methods to an existing type without modifying that type's source code — even for types you don't own (sealed classes, built-in types, third-party libraries).

Rules for creating an extension method:

  • Must be defined in a static class
  • Must be a static method
  • The first parameter specifies the type being extended, prefixed with this
// Define — static class, static method, 'this' on first param
public static class StringExtensions
{
    public static bool IsNullOrEmpty(this string s)
        => string.IsNullOrEmpty(s);

    public static string Truncate(this string s, int maxLength)
        => s.Length <= maxLength ? s : s[..maxLength] + "...";

    public static string ToTitleCase(this string s)
        => System.Globalization.CultureInfo.CurrentCulture
               .TextInfo.ToTitleCase(s.ToLower());
}

// Use — called as if it were an instance method
string name = "hello world";
Console.WriteLine(name.ToTitleCase());     // "Hello World"
Console.WriteLine(name.Truncate(5));       // "hello..."
Console.WriteLine(((string)null).IsNullOrEmpty()); // true (no NullRef!)

Where extension methods are used heavily:

  • LINQ — Where, Select, OrderBy are all extension methods on IEnumerable<T> defined in System.Linq.Enumerable.
  • IServiceCollection — AddControllers(), AddDbContext() are extension methods on IServiceCollection.
  • IApplicationBuilder — UseRouting(), UseAuthentication() are extension methods on IApplicationBuilder.

Limitations:

  • Cannot access private/protected members of the extended type.
  • If an instance method with the same signature exists, the instance method takes priority.
  • Extension methods are syntactic sugar — they are still static calls at the IL level.

followUps.question: Can extension methods be called on null references?
followUps.answer:
Yes — and this is one of their advantages. Because extension methods are really static method calls, the object can be null without throwing a NullReferenceException (as long as your extension method handles null):

public static bool IsNullOrEmpty(this string s) 
    => string.IsNullOrEmpty(s); // handles null safely

string s = null;
bool result = s.IsNullOrEmpty(); // fine — no NullReferenceException

This is why many utility extension methods do a null check on this at the start. Instance methods would throw if called on null.

questions.tip: LINQ connection: Every LINQ method (Where, Select, GroupBy) is an extension method on IEnumerable<T>. When you write list.Where(x => x > 0), the compiler translates it to Enumerable.Where(list, x => x > 0).

questions.question: What is CTS (Common Type System) and CLS (Common Language Specification) in .NET?
questions.answer:
These two are the foundation of why C# and VB.NET can work together without any issues — something I never really appreciated until I had to debug an interop issue between assemblies.

CTS — Common Type System

CTS defines all the data types and programming constructs that the CLR supports, and the rules governing how they interact. It answers questions like: what's an integer, what's a class, how does inheritance work at the runtime level. Both C# and VB.NET compile down to CTS types — so a C# int and a VB.NET Integer are literally the same CTS type: System.Int32.

CTS types fall into two buckets — value types (structs, enums, primitives) stored on the stack, and reference types (classes, interfaces, delegates) stored on the heap.

CLS — Common Language Specification

CTS is the full set of what the runtime supports. CLS is the subset that every .NET language must support if you want your library to be consumable from any .NET language. Think of CLS as the minimum contract for cross-language compatibility.

// CLS-compliant — works from C#, VB.NET, F#
public class OrderService
{
    public List<Order> GetOrders() { return new List<Order>(); }
}

// NOT CLS-compliant — unsigned types like uint, ulong are not in CLS
// VB.NET doesn't have a uint equivalent
public uint GetCount() { return 42; } // violates CLS

// Mark assembly to check compliance at compile time
[assembly: CLSCompliant(true)] // compiler will warn on violations

Real-world relevance:

  • If you're building a public NuGet library, follow CLS rules — don't expose uint, ulong, unsigned types in public APIs.
  • If you're building for your own team in C# only, you can ignore CLS entirely.
  • Most developers never think about CTS/CLS directly — the CLR handles it transparently. It matters when building SDKs or doing language interop.

followUps.question: What is the difference between CTS value types and reference types at memory level?
followUps.answer: Value types (int, bool, struct, enum) are allocated on the stack — they live and die with the method call. When you assign a value type, you get a full copy — modifying the copy doesn't affect the original. Reference types (class, string, array, delegate) are allocated on the heap — the variable holds a reference (pointer) to the heap memory. When you assign a reference type, both variables point to the same object. This is why passing a class instance to a method lets the method modify the object, but passing an int doesn't. Stack is fast and self-managing; heap requires garbage collection. Understanding this distinction explains a lot of subtle C# bugs around mutation and unexpected side effects.

questions.tip: Interview shortcut: CTS = the full type system the runtime defines. CLS = the safe subset every language understands. CLSCompliant attribute = 'please warn me if I break CLS rules in my public API'.

questions.question: What is Reflection in .NET? When have you actually used it?
questions.answer:
Reflection is the ability to inspect and interact with types, methods, properties, and attributes at runtime — without knowing them at compile time. It's powerful but slow, so I use it deliberately, not casually.

What you can do with Reflection:

// Inspect a type
Type type = typeof(OrderService);
Console.WriteLine(type.Name);          // OrderService
Console.WriteLine(type.FullName);      // MyApp.Services.OrderService
Console.WriteLine(type.IsClass);       // true

// Get all public methods
foreach (var method in type.GetMethods())
    Console.WriteLine(method.Name);

// Get properties and their values
var order = new Order { Id = 1, Status = "Pending" };
foreach (var prop in order.GetType().GetProperties())
    Console.WriteLine($"{prop.Name} = {prop.GetValue(order)}");

// Create instance dynamically — useful in plugin systems
Type serviceType = Type.GetType("MyApp.Services.EmailService");
var instance = Activator.CreateInstance(serviceType);

// Invoke a method dynamically
var method = serviceType.GetMethod("Send");
method.Invoke(instance, new object[] { "hi@email.com", "Hello" });

// Read custom attributes — this is where I use it most
[AttributeUsage(AttributeTargets.Property)]
public class AuditFieldAttribute : Attribute { }

public class User
{
    [AuditField]
    public string Email { get; set; }
    public string Password { get; set; } // not audited
}

// At runtime, find all properties marked [AuditField]
var auditableProps = typeof(User)
    .GetProperties()
    .Where(p => p.IsDefined(typeof(AuditFieldAttribute), false));
// Only Email — use this to build generic audit log, CSV export, etc.

When I've actually used it:

  • Auto-mapping — building a simple object mapper that copies matching property names between DTOs before I reached for AutoMapper.
  • Custom attribute processing — decorating model properties with [SensitiveData] and using reflection in a logging middleware to mask those fields in logs.
  • Plugin architecture — loading assemblies from a folder at runtime and discovering all classes implementing IPlugin without a hard compile-time reference.
  • Generic repository — GetById<T> using reflection to find the primary key property marked with [Key].

Performance reality:

Reflection is 10–100x slower than a direct call because it bypasses compile-time optimisations. For hot paths, cache the reflected metadata: store PropertyInfo[] in a static dictionary keyed by type — pay the reflection cost once, not on every call.

followUps.question: What is the difference between Reflection and dynamic in C#?
followUps.answer: dynamic uses the DLR (Dynamic Language Runtime) and is essentially syntactic sugar over reflection — it resolves method calls at runtime but with cleaner syntax. Reflection gives you full control: you can discover types, iterate members, read attributes. dynamic just lets you call methods without compile-time type checking. For inspecting metadata and attributes, you need reflection. For simply calling methods on an unknown type, dynamic is cleaner. Both are slow compared to static dispatch — neither belongs in tight loops.

questions.tip: Cache reflected metadata. Never call GetProperties() or GetMethod() inside a loop or per-request. Call it once at startup, store in a ConcurrentDictionary<Type, PropertyInfo[]>, reuse forever.
